summary: Cathy O'Neil announces the retirement of her advice column 'Aunt Pythia' after a long run. She provides a condensed summary of her most common advice topics: becoming a data scientist, understanding why math is difficult, dating advice, and encouraging people to act on their feelings. The column ends on a triumphant note with a reader writing back to report that following Aunt Pythia's advice to confess a long-term crush resulted in a successful relationship. The tone is warm, self-deprecating, and celebratory of the column's quirky mix of technical and personal guidance.
key_points: To become a data scientist, learn linear algebra and statistics fundamentals, build projects, and understand how to communicate technical concepts to non-technical audiences
Math feels hard because practitioners constantly push to the edge of their knowledge rather than lingering on familiar territory
Dating advice boils down to: be human, listen, make effort without desperation, and don't obsess over one person if it's not working
When you have a mutual crush and sense reciprocation, take the risk—you only live once
money_quotes: She has truly enjoyed handing out consistently bad and possibly dangerous suggestions to all you wonderful and earnest people.
Math is hard because we rarely spend time dawdling over thing we already know, however beautiful they are; instead we push ourselves to the very limits of known theory.
We are impatient and hungry for more knowledge. That's a good thing, and it's human nature, but it's also nice to dawdle sometimes.
Think about just how cool Galois Theory is every now and then.
Be a human, listen well, try to seem like you're making an effort, but don't act desperate.
There are lots of people in the world, so don't obsess with one of them if things aren't working out, but if things are working out then try to appreciate it daily.
Go for it. You only live once.
Thanks for making my advice seem good!
key_insight: The best advice—whether about math careers or love—comes down to taking informed risks, appreciating what you have, and not overthinking what you can't control.
